---
title: "Week N — Lecture Title"
subtitle: "8-Week Empirical Finance Camp"
author: "Prof. Lei Gao, George Mason University"
date: "2026-06-14"
date-format: "YYYY-MM-DD"
format:
  pptx:
    # Optional: drop a styled .pptx into book/decks/templates/ and uncomment.
    # The reference doc supplies master slide layouts, fonts, and color theme.
    # reference-doc: templates/ccli-reference.pptx
    slide-level: 2
    incremental: false
    toc: false
    fig-width: 7.5
    fig-height: 4.2
    fig-dpi: 200
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
  message: false
  freeze: auto
# bibliography: ../bibliography.md          # uncomment once book/bibliography.md exists
# csl: ../../assets/csl/chicago-author-date.csl
---

<!--
DECK TEMPLATE — usage notes for deck-drafting agents
======================================================
- One H2 (`##`) starts a new slide. Use Title Case.
- One H3 (`###`) inside a slide is an optional subhead.
- Put the speaker script inside a `::: notes` fenced div. These become
  PowerPoint speaker notes and are later fed to TTS for narration.
- Keep on-slide bullets to <= 6 lines, <= 10 words per line.
- End every content slide with a "Key takeaway" callout (see below).
- Math: use $inline$ and $$display$$ LaTeX; Pandoc converts to OMML.
- Code: triple-backtick fenced blocks with a language tag.
- Figures: drop into ../../assets/figures/ and reference by relative path.
- Citations: `[@key]` resolves against ../bibliography.md.
- Delete this comment block when authoring a real deck.
-->

# Title Slide {.title}

::: notes
Welcome to Week N of the 8-Week Empirical Finance Camp. Today we will
[one-sentence framing of the lecture's purpose]. By the end of this
session you should be able to [learning objective in plain English].
:::

## Learning Objectives

- Define [concept 1] and explain why it matters for empirical finance
- Compute [statistic / estimator] from a real dataset
- Interpret [result] in the context of [economic question]
- Critique [common pitfall] when applying the method

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
By the end of this slide deck students can move from a research question
to a defensible empirical estimate, and articulate what could go wrong.
:::

::: notes
Walk through each objective and tie it to the lab they will run after
the lecture. Mention that the dataset for today lives in
data/week-N/ and is documented in Appendix C.
:::

## Motivating Question

### Why do we care?

- Investors face [problem]
- Standard intuition says [naive answer]
- The data show [surprising fact, with citation]  [@famafrench1993]

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The empirical pattern is not what introductory theory predicts — that
gap is the whole reason this field exists.
:::

::: notes
Spend about two minutes here. Anchor the question in a recent news
story or a number students have seen in the WSJ. Do not solve it yet —
the point is to make them uncomfortable with the naive answer.
:::

## A Little Math

The excess return of asset $i$ in month $t$ is

$$
r_{i,t} - r_{f,t} = \alpha_i + \beta_i \,(r_{m,t} - r_{f,t}) + \varepsilon_{i,t},
$$

where $\beta_i = \operatorname{Cov}(r_i, r_m) / \operatorname{Var}(r_m)$.

- $\alpha_i$ — risk-adjusted "abnormal" return
- $\beta_i$ — sensitivity to the market factor
- $\varepsilon_{i,t}$ — idiosyncratic shock, mean zero

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
CAPM is a *regression*, not a law of nature. $\alpha$ is what the
model cannot explain — and that residual is where the research lives.
:::

::: notes
Walk through the equation term by term. Remind students that the
betas they will estimate in lab are sample statistics with standard
errors, not point predictions.
:::

## Code You Will Run in Lab

```{.python}
import pandas as pd
import statsmodels.api as sm

# Load Fama-French factors + a test asset (see Appendix C data card)
df = pd.read_parquet("data/week-N/ff_plus_asset.parquet")

y = df["ret"] - df["rf"]
X = sm.add_constant(df["mkt_rf"])

model = sm.OLS(y, X).fit(cov_type="HAC", cov_kwds={"maxlags": 3})
print(model.summary())
```

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
HAC standard errors matter the moment your residuals are serially
correlated — which they almost always are in monthly return data.
:::

::: notes
Do not run this live unless the room has working Python. Instead,
project the expected output (saved as a screenshot in
assets/figures/week-N/ols-summary.png) and walk through how to read
each column: coef, std err, t, p-value, confidence interval.
:::

## Result and Interpretation

![Rolling 36-month beta of the test asset](../../assets/figures/week-N/rolling-beta.png){width=70%}

- Beta drifts from `~0.8` (pre-2008) to `~1.3` (post-2020)
- Standard errors widen during 2008 and 2020 — small-sample noise
- The point estimate alone is misleading without the error band

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
A single number ("beta = 1.1") hides a time-varying, noisy estimate.
Always report uncertainty, and prefer a picture over a point estimate.
:::

::: notes
Pause here and ask: "If beta is unstable, what does that imply for a
portfolio manager who hedges using historical beta?" Let two students
answer before moving on.
:::

## Common Pitfalls

1. Survivorship bias — using only firms that exist *today*
2. Look-ahead bias — using information not available at time $t$
3. Multiple testing — running 50 regressions and reporting the best
4. Overfitting in-sample with no held-out window

::: callout-warning
## Watch out
Every one of these will make your $R^2$ look great and your out-of-sample
performance look terrible.
:::

::: notes
Tie each pitfall to a real published-then-retracted finding when you
can. Students remember stories, not bullet points.
:::

## What's Next

- Lab N: estimate $\beta$ for a stock of your choice, 2000–2025
- Read: [@famafrench1993], sections 1–3 only
- Optional: replicate Figure 2 with a different sample window

::: callout-tip
## Key takeaway
The lecture gave you the model; the lab is where you will discover
how brittle it is. That is the point.
:::

::: notes
Remind students that the lab notebook is in book/weeks/week-N/lab.ipynb
and that office hours are Thursday 4–5 PM. Close on the motivating
question from slide 2: "Has the data changed your prior yet?"
:::

## References {.unnumbered}

::: {#refs}
:::

::: notes
The references slide is auto-populated by Pandoc from cited keys.
Do not edit by hand.
:::
